title: Summer pig management: shade and cooling
subtitle: Preventing heat stress in outdoor pigs during hot weather.
excerpt: Pigs cannot sweat. They lose heat only through their skin surface and breathing, which makes them highly vulnerable to heat stress above 25 degrees Celsius. A pig in heat stress stops eating, pants heavily, and lies stretched out and flat. Prolonged heat stress causes death. Shade, wallows, and adjusted feeding times are the three tools that prevent it.
sourceNotes:
AHDB Pork, Heat stress in pigs guidance. https://ahdb.org.uk
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Pigs (2003). https://www.gov.uk
Nadis, Heat stress in pigs. https://www.nadis.org.uk

glossaryTerms.definition: A condition in which a pig cannot maintain its core body temperature within a safe range. Signs include open-mouth panting, skin reddening, reluctance to move, reduced feed intake, and lying stretched flat on the ground. Severe heat stress leads to organ failure and death if not addressed quickly.

A shallow muddy area or purpose-made trough in which pigs coat themselves in wet mud. The mud layer protects against sunburn and provides cooling by evaporation. A functional wallow is the single most effective heat-management tool for outdoor pigs.

The ambient temperature range in which a pig can maintain body temperature without extra metabolic effort. For a growing pig of 30 to 60 kg, this is roughly 16 to 22 degrees Celsius. Above this range, a pig is under increasing heat load; below it, extra feed energy is needed for warmth.

content.text: on most UK summer afternoons. Unlike cattle or horses, pigs have no functional sweat glands. They rely entirely on behavioural cooling: seeking shade, spreading out on cool ground, or wallowing in mud. If none of those options are available,

Shade must be large enough for all pigs to lie under at the same time. A pig arc without ventilation becomes a heat trap in direct sun. Raise one side of the arc slightly or add a separate shade structure. Corrugated metal roofs absorb and radiate heat; shade cloth or a tarp stretched over a wooden frame is much cooler.

A tree canopy is the best shade of all. If your paddock has no trees, position a shade tarp on the south-west side of the paddock. Afternoon sun in the UK comes from the south-west, so a tarp on that side gives shade in the hottest hours.

is the most effective cooling tool for outdoor pigs. It can be as simple as a low-sided rubber trug or a dug-out area kept wet with a hose. Ideally it is 30 to 40 cm deep and wide enough for all pigs to use at once. Refresh the water daily in hot weather. A wallow that has dried out and baked hard provides no cooling benefit.

Pigs coat themselves in mud to protect against sunburn as well as to cool down. Light-coloured breeds such as Large White and Landrace burn quickly. A pig with pink, peeling skin on its back has been sunburned. Once a wallow is established, most pigs use it routinely in warm weather.

A garden misting nozzle attached to the water supply and aimed at the resting area works well for small groups. Run it in short cycles during the hottest part of the day (roughly noon to 4 pm in the UK). Continuous misting in humid conditions raises humidity without cooling and can worsen respiratory stress; short bursts every 30 minutes are more effective.

Digestion generates body heat. Do not feed in the hottest hours of the day. Move the main feed to early morning and late evening. Pigs eat better in cooler conditions and reduce feed wastage. A pig that has not eaten its morning ration by mid-morning on a hot day is already showing reduced appetite from heat load.

attrs.title: Signs of heat stress requiring immediate action
attrs.body: A pig lying flat and stretched, breathing rapidly with an open mouth, and not responding to approach is in acute heat stress. Move it to shade immediately. Apply cool (not cold) water to the head and neck. Offer fresh water but do not force it. If a pig does not respond within 20 minutes of cooling attempts, call your vet.

content.text: Water demand almost doubles in hot weather. A pig that normally drinks 6 litres per day may need 10 to 12 litres at 30 degrees Celsius. Check that troughs are full at every inspection. A pig that cannot access water for two hours in hot weather becomes dehydrated quickly. Valve-fed troughs can block with algae in summer; flush them daily.
